narration: What if your dog isn’t just begging… but trying to tell you something?

under_narration.caption: a dog is looking at a toy on the floor
a small dog standing on top of a wooden floor

segments.transcript: [4.36s-14.44s] [SPEAKER_14] With a new tool in their communication kit and access to our words, perhaps the close bond between humans and dogs could become even closer.

[1.59s-3.00s] [SPEAKER_14] So what's really going | [3.00s-3.85s] [SPEAKER_14] going on here? | [4.96s-5.35s] [SPEAKER_14] Are they just | [5.35s-6.49s] [SPEAKER_14] just pushing our buttons? | [8.47s-8.61s] [SPEAKER_14] Or | [8.61s-10.40s] [SPEAKER_14] Or can dogs talk?

narration: We’ve always talked to dogs. The twist is… now some dogs are talking back—with our own words.

under_narration.caption: a black background with the words can dogs talk?
a white dog sitting on top of a table

[4.57s-4.71s] [SPEAKER_06] Button. | [4.73s-4.86s] [SPEAKER_06] Treat. | [4.87s-5.15s] [SPEAKER_06] Yeah, so good. | [5.18s-5.26s] [SPEAKER_06] Go get | [5.26s-5.40s] [SPEAKER_06] get it.
[0.43s-8.94s] [SPEAKER_14] From the data being submitted by dog owners, Federico has found that many believe their dogs are using buttons to do more than just ask for things.

narration: But here’s the problem: a button press can look like a conversation… even when it’s just a trick.

segments.transcript: [42.92s-45.03s] [SPEAKER_03] So what we saw looked like a conversation where | [45.03s-47.79s] [SPEAKER_03] where the dog understood what it was saying and we had a back and forth. | [48.61s-50.01s] [SPEAKER_03] In actual fact, these were trained | [50.01s-53.68s] [SPEAKER_03] trained interactions where the dog was actually just trained to press the buttons as a trick.

[0.85s-4.20s] [SPEAKER_14] Rohan has learned to associate an action, like pressing | [4.20s-7.28s] [SPEAKER_14] pressing a button, with an outcome, like getting a treat. | [7.96s-11.58s] [SPEAKER_14] He likes treats, so he keeps pressing the buttons. | [12.76s-16.37s] [SPEAKER_14] It's known as associative learning, or conditioning. | [17.48s-20.12s] [SPEAKER_14] But it doesn't mean he knows what the words treat, or | [20.12s-22.59s] [SPEAKER_14] thanks, or love you actually mean.

narration: So how do you tell the difference between “trained behavior”… and real understanding?

segments.transcript: [0.00s-2.07s] [SPEAKER_03] Everything in life starts through associative learning, and | [2.07s-3.63s] [SPEAKER_03] and actually humans learn through associative learning. | [4.21s-4.85s] [SPEAKER_03] Good boy. | [4.87s-4.88s] [SPEAKER_03] Associative | [4.88s-8.12s] [SPEAKER_03] Associative learning is common to pretty much all animals. | [8.58s-8.84s] Freak! | [9.46s-9.84s] [SPEAKER_03] And it doesn't | [9.84s-13.67s] [SPEAKER_03] doesn't require any sort of complex cognition that we should be impressed by.

[23.28s-24.56s] [SPEAKER_13] We know that dogs can respond | [24.56s-29.53s] [SPEAKER_13] appropriately to words, but that could just be conditioning, that could just be training. | [29.79s-34.75s] [SPEAKER_13] So whether or not they understand what the word itself means is much harder to test.

narration: To answer that, researchers went big: thousands of dogs, across the world, all pressing the same kinds of word buttons.

[0.00s-0.78s] [SPEAKER_14] Federico and | [0.78s-1.68s] [SPEAKER_14] Amalia have been recruiting | [1.68s-2.61s] [SPEAKER_14] recruiting participants | [2.61s-3.41s] [SPEAKER_14] participants from around | [3.41s-4.00s] [SPEAKER_14] around the world. | [7.16s-9.05s] [SPEAKER_14] While early contributors in the study used | [9.05s-11.98s] [SPEAKER_14] used simple spreadsheets to log their dogs' button presses, | [11.98s-15.63s] [SPEAKER_14] presses, many owners are now using buttons that automatically | [15.63s-18.78s] [SPEAKER_14] automatically record which words are pressed and when.

[2.57s-7.16s] [SPEAKER_14] We know that some dogs seem to make sense some of the time with their button presses. | [8.44s-9.24s] [SPEAKER_14] But the data that | [9.24s-13.27s] [SPEAKER_14] Federico is collecting will help his team understand the bigger picture. | [14.05s-14.52s] [SPEAKER_14] Do the thousands | [14.52s-17.75s] [SPEAKER_14] thousands of dogs taking part know what they're doing with buttons? | [18.51s-20.47s] [SPEAKER_14] Or is most of it just random?

narration: And then there are the dogs that make owners swear it’s more than asking for snacks—like they’re narrating life.

segments.transcript: [0.99s-1.65s] [SPEAKER_14] Meet Parker. | [2.76s-6.30s] [SPEAKER_14] She's a beagle cross and a prolific user of word buttons.

[21.91s-24.58s] [SPEAKER_18] Parker uses the buttons to narrate a decent amount. | [24.70s-28.62s] [SPEAKER_18] She has narrated me watering the plants quite a bit. | [29.08s-31.15s] [SPEAKER_18] She'll narrate also things she does sometimes. | [34.95s-40.22s] [SPEAKER_18] She's pressed settle before and then just gone into her bed and lie down. | [41.74s-41.81s] [SPEAKER_18] She | [41.81s-43.46s] [SPEAKER_18] She didn't get anything from me for it. | [43.90s-45.05s] [SPEAKER_18] She just pressed settle, | [45.05s-45.84s] [SPEAKER_18] and then she settled.

narration: But the most tempting moments are the emotional ones—when a dog seems to talk about time, plans… even feelings.

under_narration.caption: a black background with the words can dogs talk?
a person petting a dog in a field

segments.transcript: [56.07s-58.49s] [SPEAKER_14] But other dogs, like Copper, a three-and-a-half-year-old | [58.49s-63.40s] [SPEAKER_14] three-and-a-half-year-old Labrador, also seem to be using buttons to do more than just make requests. | [64.30s-65.20s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool. | [65.23s-66.09s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool. | [66.63s-69.23s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool, maybe tomorrow. | [74.44s-75.60s] [SPEAKER_09] Outside pool later. | [77.45s-77.87s] [SPEAKER_09] Later. | [81.96s-83.48s] [SPEAKER_09] Oh my goodness, copper. | [84.75s-87.49s] [SPEAKER_14] It looks like copper was using her buttons to express | [87.49s-88.62s] [SPEAKER_14] express emotion.
[7.67s-8.75s] [SPEAKER_14] But scientific understanding | [8.75s-12.77s] [SPEAKER_14] understanding of their linguistic capabilities is still in its early days.

narration: So maybe the real story isn’t buttons at all. It’s the ancient skill dogs already have: reading us—our gestures, our gaze, our tone.

under_narration.caption: a dog playing with a puzzle board on the floor
a dog with a toy in it ' s mouth

segments.transcript: [14.76s-16.36s] [SPEAKER_02] In addition to word forms, dogs | [16.36s-19.72s] [SPEAKER_02] dogs are also paying attention to the way our body moves. | [20.01s-21.83s] [SPEAKER_02] They're paying attention to where we're looking. | [22.09s-25.82s] [SPEAKER_02] They're paying attention if we're pointing at things or if we're gesturing at things. | [26.64s-29.34s] [SPEAKER_02] They're paying attention to the tone of our voice. | [29.38s-30.11s] [SPEAKER_02] So | [30.11s-35.48s] [SPEAKER_02] So they're reading things off of us all the time, you know, even as subtle as an arm gesture. | [35.48s-35.52s] [SPEAKER_02] gesture.

narration: That talent didn’t appear overnight. Dogs evolved beside us—shaped by thousands of years of living close enough to understand what we mean.

under_narration.caption: a woman sitting on the floor playing with her dog
a small dog standing on top of a wooden floor

segments.transcript: [26.81s-30.55s] [SPEAKER_14] All dogs evolved from wolves, their wild ancestors. | [31.61s-33.06s] [SPEAKER_14] More than 10,000 years ago, | [33.06s-36.27s] [SPEAKER_14] ago, some wolves were taken in by humans and domesticated. | [36.27s-36.52s] [SPEAKER_14] domesticated. | [36.56s-37.24s] [SPEAKER_14] Over | [37.24s-38.21s] [SPEAKER_14] Over | [38.21s-39.14s] [SPEAKER_14] Over generations | [39.14s-40.01s] [SPEAKER_14] generations spent | [40.01s-40.94s] [SPEAKER_14] living with and | [40.94s-42.01s] [SPEAKER_14] and around us, | [42.01s-42.88s] [SPEAKER_14] they evolved in | [42.88s-43.71s] [SPEAKER_14] in different ways | [43.71s-44.68s] [SPEAKER_14] ways according | [44.68s-45.64s] [SPEAKER_14] according to how we bred them. | [45.64s-45.80s] [SPEAKER_14] them.

[60.59s-61.06s] [SPEAKER_14] And of course, | [61.06s-65.53s] [SPEAKER_14] course, over time, we favored dogs that could communicate well with us. | [66.55s-69.90s] [SPEAKER_14] Dogs that were good at understanding what we're trying to say.

narration: And when that understanding clicks, it becomes teamwork—human and dog moving as one, in ways no other relationship can match.

under_narration.caption: a person petting a dog in a field
a woman playing with a dog on the floor

segments.transcript: [19.63s-20.88s] [SPEAKER_02] I think the human-dog relationship | [20.88s-23.64s] [SPEAKER_02] relationship is so special.
[0.00s-6.77s] [SPEAKER_02] we work together as human-dog teams is just unlike any other relationship

narration: So here’s the payoff: whether or not dogs are truly “using language,” the real miracle is that they keep meeting us halfway—again and again—until we understand each other.
